headline: Import Permit System for Rice Generates RM800M in Rent-Seeking
context: A MITI review found that Malaysia's approved permit (AP) system for rice imports generates an estimated RM800 million annually in economic rent for 42 AP holders, who profit from the gap between world market prices and controlled domestic prices.

cards.big: 42 AP holders pocket RM800 million yearly from rice import permits — a licence to print money at consumers' expense
cards.sub: Import permit creates artificial scarcity: domestic rice at RM3.20/kg while world price equivalent is RM2.10/kg

cards.big: Rice AP system adds RM1.10 per kg to consumer prices — costing Malaysian households RM2.8 billion annually in higher food costs
cards.sub: Removing AP system would reduce rice prices 25-30% overnight — but politically connected permit holders resist reform

cards.big: B40 households spend 8.2% of income on rice versus 1.4% for T20 — AP-inflated prices are a regressive tax on the poor
cards.sub: Average family of 4 consumes 12kg of rice monthly — AP system costs them RM158 more per year than open market would

cards.big: AP allocation criteria are undisclosed — MITI issues permits through administrative discretion with no public tender or rotation
cards.sub: Same 42 companies have held rice APs for average of 18 years — no new entrants permitted since 2014

cards.big: An import permit that enriches 42 companies while making rice more expensive for 33 million people isn't food security — it's a food tax
Should rice import permits be abolished and replaced with an open import system with quality and safety standards only?
